<summary>Redacted codex session transcript</summary>

````text
source: [LOCAL_SESSION]
redaction: local paths, emails, phone-shaped strings, token-shaped strings, auth headers, auth query params
omitted: raw tool outputs, system/developer prompts, local paths, secrets, browser/session/auth details
stats: {"agent":"codex","entries":11,"user":5,"assistant":5,"toolCalls":331,"toolOutputsDropped":331,"web":0,"redactions":4,"omittedUnsafe":0,"rawEntries":94}

[user]
Setup a worktree for this issue: [https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/88196](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/88196)

[assistant]
I’ll use the worktree workflow, first checking the source checkout state and the issue details so the new worktree is based on the right branch and name.

I’m using the `using-git-worktrees` skill here. The checkout is clean on `release/[REDACTED_PHONE]`; next I’m checking whether this is already an isolated worktree, syncing refs, and reading issue 88196 for a sane branch name.

Issue 88196 is open and high priority, with labels saying it needs maintainer review/live repro before automated repair. I’m going to create an isolated branch from the current upstream base after refreshing refs, leaving the existing release checkout untouched.

The upstream refresh pulled `origin/main` forward and left the current release checkout up to date. `git push --dry-run` against `origin` failed with a 403 for this account, so I’ll keep this purely local and base the worktree on refreshed `origin/main`.

There are already several project worktrees under `workspace-scratch`, so I’m using the same outside-repo location rather than adding a project-local `.worktrees` entry that is not currently ignored. Branch name: `abs/88196-codex-app-server-timeouts`.

The worktree is created at `origin/main` commit `023a101b91`. I’m checking its baseline state and whether dependencies are already present before doing any install or test setup.

The new worktree is clean and has no `node_modules`. I’m installing with the repo’s default `pnpm` setup before running any narrow baseline proof.

Dependencies installed cleanly. I’m reading the narrow scoped guidance for the Codex/app-server and embedded-agent test surfaces before running a small baseline.

The relevant scoped guidance points to the same two narrow files called out in the issue review. I’m running those through the worktree-safe Vitest wrapper rather than direct `pnpm test`.

The narrow baseline passed: two Vitest shards, 60 tests total. I’m doing the final cleanliness check now so the worktree is ready to use.

Worktree is ready.

Branch: `abs/88196-codex-app-server-timeouts` tracking `origin/main` at `023a101b91`.
From the repo root, enter it with:

```bash

```

Setup completed with `pnpm install`. Narrow baseline passed:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.turn-watches.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.codex-app-server-recovery.test.ts
```

Result: 2 Vitest shards passed, 60 tests passed. Original release checkout is still clean. One note: `git push --dry-run` to `origin` hit a 403 for this account, so the setup is local-only for now.

https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/88196

::git-create-branch{cwd="[LOCAL_PATH]" branch="abs/88196-codex-app-server-timeouts"}

I’ll use the `fleet-dev` skill for the OpenClaw source workflow, then inspect the issue/worktree before touching code so the fix targets the actual regression rather than only the earlier timeout symptoms.

The issue text confirms this is broader than cron now: a Discord turn can hit the same app-server timeout/final-delivery loss. I’m tracing the current watchdog/retry contract and comparing it with nearby release changes before deciding the patch shape.

The current owner boundary has two distinct timers: a long attempt-progress watchdog based on `agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds`, and a much shorter completion-idle watchdog defaulting to 60 seconds after app-server turn activity. The issue timings fit a post-tool completion-idle abort more than a total-run timeout, so I’m checking recent commits and shipped defaults before patching that contract.

I’m going to patch the watchdog owner boundary, not the cron layer: after a Codex tool handoff, native tool completion, or post-tool raw progress, the same post-tool idle budget should hold both the completion guard and the progress watchdog. That keeps long silent synthesis from being cut off by the old 60s completion window or the base attempt-progress timeout.

The patch now extends both timers for dynamic tool responses and native tool completions. I’m running the focused Codex watchdog tests first; if they expose old assumptions in nearby cases, I’ll adjust those tests to the new owner contract.

The first targeted shard is still running longer than normal, which likely means one of the modified watchdog expectations is hanging on a timer path. I’m waiting for the concrete failure before changing more.

Two existing tests were intentionally asserting a very short post-tool timeout via the old `turnCompletionIdleTimeoutMs` path. I updated them to set the post-tool timeout explicitly, which keeps the old stall tests meaningful under the new contract.

The focused Codex watchdog shard is green now with the new regression cases. I’m running the embedded-agent recovery shard next to make sure the higher-level retry/surfacing behavior still matches the app-server failure contract.

The app-server behavior tests are passing. I’m formatting the touched docs/source files now, then I’ll rerun the focused proof because formatting can expose table/doc wrapping or TypeScript style changes.

Formatting completed and the docs formatter rewrote the app-server option table alignment around the updated timeout text. I’m rerunning the focused two-shard proof after formatting.

The post-format focused proof is green: 62 tests across the Codex watchdog and embedded-agent recovery shards. I’m doing the final repo sanity checks now: dependency-file scan, whitespace diff check, and current file list.

Sanity checks are clean: no dependency-file diff, no whitespace errors, and the touched set is limited to Codex app-server runtime/tests/docs/manifest. I’m going to run the repo’s autoreview workflow next, per the OpenClaw pre-land rule for non-trivial code changes.

Autoreview is running and has not emitted findings yet. I’ll let the helper continue; this command can take several minutes while the Codex reviewer inspects the local patch.

The autoreview heartbeat is healthy at 120 seconds. I’m continuing to wait for the structured result rather than interrupting a live review.

Autoreview is still active at 240 seconds with normal heartbeats. While it runs, I’m not changing files; a finished review needs to correspond to the current diff.

The review helper is now at 6 minutes and still progressing. I’ll keep waiting for the structured clean/finding result; stopping here would leave the review gate inconclusive.

Autoreview is at 10 minutes with heartbeats, which is still within the skill’s normal window for Codex review. I’m continuing to wait.

Autoreview found one real issue: buffered notifications that arrive while `turn/start` is in flight can arm the new post-tool budget, then a later `turn:start` progress touch resets it back to the short timeout. I’m going to move/fix that ordering and add coverage for the buffered-notification path.

I moved the attempt-progress `turn:start` touch before buffered notification replay, so replayed tool-completion notifications now remain authoritative. I also added a regression test for native tool completion emitted during `turn/start`.

The reviewer finding is fixed and the focused tests are green again, now 63 tests across the two shards. I’m rerunning autoreview on the updated local diff; this is required because the review-triggered fix changed code.

The second autoreview is running with a normal heartbeat at 60 seconds. I’ll wait for the structured result before doing final checks.

Autoreview found two more real owner-boundary misses: raw `custom_tool_call_output` was being captured by the generic raw item branch before the post-tool branch, and dynamic-tool `item/completed` bookkeeping was clearing the override after the tool response. I’m fixing both and strengthening the existing tests so different short/long budgets expose regressions.

I corrected both review misses: raw tool-output completions now bypass the generic raw-item branch and use the post-tool budget, and dynamic-tool completion bookkeeping explicitly rearms the post-tool guard after its activity touch. The tests now use different short/long timeout values so these cases can’t pass accidentally.

The second round of fixes is formatted and the focused test proof is still green. I’m rerunning autoreview again; this should be the final review pass if no new actionable findings remain.

The third autoreview is at 60 seconds with a normal heartbeat. I’m waiting for the result.

Autoreview is still healthy at 4 minutes. I’m continuing to let it finish so the closeout has a real clean review result or concrete findings.

Autoreview’s accepted/actionable result is clean, but it surfaced an ignored docs mismatch in the primary Codex harness page. Since this is user-visible config behavior, I’m going to update that page too rather than leaving the reference and primary docs inconsistent.

I updated the primary Codex harness docs to match the reference and manifest behavior. I’m formatting docs and then I’ll rerun the relevant proof/review one more time because the reviewed diff changed.

I’m picking up in the issue-specific worktree and doing the closeout checks after the docs alignment change. I’ll verify the diff shape first, then rerun formatting, focused tests, autoreview, and sanity checks.

The diff is limited to Codex harness docs, plugin metadata, app-server watchdog code, and the focused watchdog tests. I’m formatting the two docs now, then I’ll rerun the targeted regression suite.

The focused suite is green: 2 Vitest shards, 63 tests. I’m running autoreview again now because the reviewed diff changed after the docs correction.

The absolute autoreview script path from the prior run is not present in this environment; that command failed before doing any work. I’m locating the repo-local autoreview entrypoint and will run the same local mode through the available script.

Autoreview is still running; no findings have printed yet. I’m waiting for it to finish before the final diff checks.

The autoreview worker is taking longer than the tests, which is expected for this repo-local review pass. I’m keeping it open rather than starting overlapping review or test commands in the same worktree.

Autoreview is still active with no output beyond the progress heartbeat. I’m giving it the full chance to finish because this is the gate most likely to catch cross-surface issues after the docs/code changes.

Still waiting on the repo-local autoreview. There are no actionable findings reported so far; I’ll only proceed to final sanity checks once this process exits or clearly fails.

The review pass is past four minutes but still emitting its heartbeat, so it has not hung at the shell level. I’m letting it continue; starting a second pass would make the result less clear.

Autoreview is now a long-running check. I’m going to wait a bit longer for a clean exit; if it exceeds a reasonable window, I’ll report that specific gate as blocked and keep the other validation evidence precise.

Autoreview finished clean with no accepted or actionable findings. I’m running the last mechanical checks now: whitespace, dependency-surface scan, status, and final diff size.

Implemented the issue fix on `abs/88196-codex-app-server-timeouts`.

The Codex app-server now keeps both the completion-idle guard and the attempt-progress watchdog on `appServer.postToolRawAssistantCompletionIdleTimeoutMs` after tool handoff paths, including OpenClaw dynamic tool responses, native tool completion, raw tool-output completion, post-tool raw assistant progress, and buffered notifications during `turn/start`. Docs and plugin metadata now describe that contract consistently.

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.turn-watches.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.codex-app-server-recovery.test.ts` passed, 2 shards / 63 tests
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local` clean, no accepted/actionable findings

I did not run a live cron/Discord Codex app-server repro; the proof is source-level plus focused regression coverage. The issue is still open upstream.

https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/88196

[user]
How can we test it on live? I have a idle discord bot can do the test, the "Crabboxer"

[assistant]
I’ll use the OpenClaw testing and Crabbox guidance here, because live proof needs to exercise the real Discord-to-agent path, not just rerun the Vitest coverage.

The key constraint is that we should not test against your normal gateway in-place. The clean live proof is a canary OpenClaw home with only Crabboxer’s Discord token/channel and the patched Codex plugin, so a failure cannot disturb the rest of the fleet.

I checked the local docs/scripts. The repo runner we want is `pnpm openclaw ...`; the live Discord setup docs support an env-backed `DISCORD_BOT_TOKEN`, and the key proof window after gateway restart should use a longer health timeout because Discord reconnect can lag.

Yes. Use Crabboxer as a canary Discord bot and make the timeout window small so the old bug would fail quickly.

Recommended live proof:

1. Run a patched canary gateway from this worktree, not the managed production gateway.
2. Configure only Crabboxer’s Discord token, one guild/channel, and one allowed user.
3. Set:
   - `appServer.turnCompletionIdleTimeoutMs: 3000`
   - `appServer.postToolRawAssistantCompletionIdleTimeoutMs: 90000`
4. Send a Discord prompt that forces Codex to use a native tool.
5. Pass criteria: final Discord reply appears, and logs do not contain `codex app-server attempt timed out` or `turn idle timed out waiting for turn/completed`.

Test prompt:

```text
Use the shell tool first: printf 'crabboxer-live-proof-88196\n'
After the tool returns, reply exactly:
CRABBOXER_LIVE_88196_OK
```

If the old path is still active, the short 3s completion guard is likely to trip after the tool result. With the patch, the post-tool guard should take over and give Codex up to 90s to synthesize the final reply.

For setup, the safest path is an isolated canary config/state:

```bash
CANARY_ROOT="$(mktemp -d)"
export OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH="$CANARY_ROOT/openclaw.json"
export OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR="$CANARY_ROOT/state"
export DISCORD_BOT_TOKEN="set-this-in-shell-only"

pnpm openclaw models auth login --provider openai
```

Then patch config with Crabboxer’s guild/channel/user IDs and the Codex timeout override:

```bash
cat > crabboxer-live.patch.json5 <<'JSON5'
{
  gateway: {
    port: 19796,
  },
  plugins: {
    allow: ["codex"],
    entries: {
      codex: {
        enabled: true,
        config: {
          appServer: {
            mode: "yolo",
            approvalPolicy: "never",
            sandbox: "danger-full-access",
            turnCompletionIdleTimeoutMs: 3000,
            postToolRawAssistantCompletionIdleTimeoutMs: 90000,
          },
        },
      },
    },
  },
  channels: {
    discord: {
      enabled: true,
      token: { source: "env", provider: "default", id: "DISCORD_BOT_TOKEN" },
      groupPolicy: "allowlist",
      guilds: {
        "DISCORD_GUILD_ID": {
          requireMention: true,
          users: ["DISCORD_USER_ID"],
          channels: {
            "DISCORD_TEST_CHANNEL_ID": { allow: true, requireMention: true },
          },
        },
      },
    },
  },
}
JSON5

pnpm openclaw config patch --file crabboxer-live.patch.json5 --dry-run
pnpm openclaw config patch --file crabboxer-live.patch.json5
pnpm openclaw config validate
pnpm openclaw gateway --port 19796
```

If you want me to run it, set the token in the shell only and give me the non-secret guild ID, channel ID, user ID, and whether the canary needs a fresh Codex login or can use an API-key fallback.

[user]
I have set the bot token guild id user id and test channel id all in the zshenv
and no need a fresh codex login

[assistant]
I’ll run this as an isolated canary gateway using the env you put in `zshenv`, and I’ll only verify that the variables are present, not echo their values. Then I’ll send one Discord message that forces a Codex tool turn and check the logs for the old timeout signatures.

The env has the four expected Discord variables present. One caveat: with an isolated OpenClaw state, Codex auth profiles may not exist there; I’m checking the local auth/state shape without printing secrets so the canary can reuse only what it needs.

The CLI help runs overlapped and one command hit a stale dist chunk while TypeScript artifacts were rebuilding. I’m going to run a single build now before starting the canary gateway, so the live path is not polluted by a local build-race failure.

The main agent already has an OpenAI Codex OAuth profile ordered under `auth.order.openai`, so I can reuse that profile inside the canary state without a new login. I’ll copy only the auth profile file into a private temp state directory and keep the canary config separate from your normal gateway.

The first canary config validation caught one schema mismatch: Discord channel entries do not accept an `allow` flag. I’m correcting that to the documented shape with the channel present and `requireMention` set.

The canary config validates. I’m starting the gateway on port `19796` now; once Discord reports ready, I’ll trigger the turn.

Gateway startup is correctly fail-closed because the canary config did not declare `gateway.mode`. I’m adding `gateway.mode="local"` and restarting the same canary.

The canary gateway is live and Discord resolved the Crabboxer test channel. I’m waiting for Discord command setup to settle, then I’ll run a health check before triggering the actual turn.

The canary started with a generated one-time gateway token, which prevents local CLI health/agent commands from connecting because the token is not persisted. For this isolated loopback-only canary, I’m switching gateway auth to `none` so the local proof command can drive the live Gateway directly.

Health is green: Crabboxer is connected, `codex` and `discord` are the only loaded plugins, and the canary default agent is `main`. I’m checking the CLI agent command now to see whether we can drive the turn from the Gateway and deliver it to the Crabboxer channel without needing you to manually type the prompt.

I can drive the live turn through the Gateway CLI and deliver the final answer to the Crabboxer Discord channel. This proves the patched Codex app-server + Discord delivery path; it does not prove Discord inbound parsing unless you also send a manual message from your user account afterward.

The live Gateway-driven turn completed and delivered to Discord. It used Codex (`agentHarnessId=codex`), ran one `bash` tool call, returned `CRABBOXER_LIVE_88196_OK`, and delivery status is `sent`. I’m checking the gateway logs now for the old timeout signatures before calling the live proof clean.

The Gateway-driven live proof is clean: no timeout signatures in the log, and the Discord API confirms the `CRABBOXER_LIVE_88196_OK` bot message landed in the test channel at `2026-05-30T11:55:19.330Z`.

If you want the extra inbound Discord proof, send this from your allowed Discord user in the Crabboxer test channel now:

```text
@Crabboxer Use the shell tool first. Run exactly: printf 'crabboxer-inbound-proof-88196\n'. After the tool returns, reply exactly: CRABBOXER_INBOUND_88196_OK
```
````

target.title: fix(agents): avoid full stream replay on text deltas
target.body:
## Summary
- avoid reparsing the full accumulated assistant stream for ordinary plain text deltas in the embedded subscriber
- keep full reply-directive parsing for `text_end`, phase-aware replies, silent tokens, media/reply/audio directives, and streams whose prior raw text was already cleaned
- add regressions for held directive tails and stripped directive metadata staying out of later deltas

Relates to https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/86599.

## Verification
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD` - passed after final rebase to `da7fb64aa48`
- `node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.messages.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.messages.test.ts` - passed
- `node_modules/.bin/oxlint src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.messages.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.messages.test.ts` - passed
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.messages.test.ts --reporter=dot` - 1 file / 33 tests passed after final rebase
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main` - clean, no accepted/actionable findings; `overall: patch is correct (0.86)`
- AWS Crabbox: `node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider aws --idle-timeout 60m --ttl 180m --timing-json --shell -- "pnpm check:changed"`; provider `aws`, lease `cbx_728ab79ceb29`, slug `brisk-barnacle`, run `run_fed87d48fbba`, machine `c7a.8xlarge`, exit `0`, `leaseStopped=true`

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: Plain assistant text deltas no longer force full accumulated reply-directive parsing on every chunk while directive-bearing, final, and phase-aware paths keep the canonical full parse.
Real environment tested: Focused local Vitest wrapper, formatting/lint checks, autoreview, and AWS Crabbox changed gate.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: See the Verification section above.
Evidence after fix: Focused subscriber regression tests pass after rebasing onto current `origin/main`; AWS Crabbox `pnpm check:changed` passed on the refreshed branch.
Observed result after fix: Ordinary plain deltas reuse the incremental visible delta instead of replaying the full accumulated stream, while incomplete directive tails and previously stripped directives stay suppressed.
What was not tested: Live Ollama/API runtime was not rerun for this focused subscriber hot-path PR.

target.title: fix(update): guard Windows task autostart during package updates
target.body:
## Summary
- disables enabled Windows Scheduled Task autostart while a managed package update replaces the OpenClaw package tree
- covers running, stopped, and unclassified installed task runtimes, including `--no-restart` stopped/unknown cases where the task can still logon-start during replacement
- re-enables only tasks this update actually disabled, before post-core handoff, restart handoff, blocked-update exits, or failed-update exits
- reports failed `/ENABLE` recovery if service stop fails after autostart was suspended, so the operator is not left with silently disabled logon autostart
- leaves startup-folder fallback installs, unavailable `schtasks`, missing tasks, unreadable XML state, and already-disabled tasks unchanged

Fixes #87993.

## Verification
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/update-cli.test.ts -- -t "Windows Scheduled Task autostart|post-core fresh-process|failed Windows Scheduled Task resume"` - 7 passed, 126 skipped
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/update-cli.test.ts src/daemon/schtasks.stop.test.ts` - 146 passed
- `node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check src/daemon/schtasks.ts src/daemon/schtasks.stop.test.ts src/cli/update-cli/update-command.ts src/cli/update-cli.test.ts`
- `node_modules/.bin/oxlint src/daemon/schtasks.ts src/daemon/schtasks.stop.test.ts src/cli/update-cli/update-command.ts src/cli/update-cli.test.ts`
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD && git diff --check`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main` - clean, patch correct
- GitHub quick checks on `dfc06a56ea882f81ffa2c877d1eebb9ac37369fe`: `Real behavior proof`, `Workflow Sanity / no-tabs`, and `Workflow Sanity / actionlint` passed
- Current Windows/WSL probe on `dfc06a56ea882f81ffa2c877d1eebb9ac37369fe`: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/26689981288
  - Native Windows probe: passed on a Blacksmith Windows Server 2025 runner.
  - WSL2 probe: `wsl.exe` existed and feature enabling ran, but distro VM creation/import still failed on the runner; the enforced WSL2 gate failed with `OPENCLAW_WSL2_PROBE_OK=false`.
- Previous native Windows CI on `52fc3efe6d88d2f90e86eeec66cab0247834ab73`: `checks-windows-node-test` passed in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/26687031872/job/78656917533
- Direct Azure Crabbox seeded/native Windows package-update proof remains blocked by provider capacity from the earlier attempts (`run_b9f14cd41731`, `run_bb068f4b20a5`).

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: Windows package updates no longer leave an enabled Scheduled Task free to relaunch the gateway while the package tree is being replaced. If the task was suspended and service stop then fails, failed `/ENABLE` recovery is now surfaced instead of hidden.

Real environment tested: Focused local regression tests on current SHA `dfc06a56ea882f81ffa2c877d1eebb9ac37369fe`; current GitHub native Windows probe on a Blacksmith Windows Server 2025 runner; WSL2 capability probe on that same Windows runner; previous native Windows CI on the pre-recovery-fix SHA.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```sh
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/update-cli.test.ts -- -t "Windows Scheduled Task autostart|post-core fresh-process|failed Windows Scheduled Task resume"
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/update-cli.test.ts src/daemon/schtasks.stop.test.ts
node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check src/daemon/schtasks.ts src/daemon/schtasks.stop.test.ts src/cli/update-cli/update-command.ts src/cli/update-cli.test.ts
node_modules/.bin/oxlint src/daemon/schtasks.ts src/daemon/schtasks.stop.test.ts src/cli/update-cli/update-command.ts src/cli/update-cli.test.ts
git diff --check origin/main...HEAD && git diff --check
.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main
ghx workflow run windows-testbox-probe.yml --ref main -f target_ref=dfc06a56ea882f81ffa2c877d1eebb9ac37369fe -f runner_label=blacksmith-16vcpu-windows-2025 -f keepalive_minutes=0 -f require_wsl2=true -f import_ubuntu_wsl2=true -f enable_wsl2_features=true
```

Evidence after fix: tests assert `/DISABLE` happens before service stop/package install and `/ENABLE` happens after install, before post-core fresh-process handoff; tests also cover stopped and unknown installed task runtimes, `--no-restart` stopped/unknown runtimes, already-disabled task preservation, startup-fallback/no-schtasks no-op, Settings-level XML parsing, NUL-separated XML output, and failed `/ENABLE` recovery after a service-stop failure. Autoreview reported no actionable findings. Current quick checks passed. Current native Windows probe passed checkout/environment on the pushed SHA; WSL2 remains unavailable on the runner.

Observed result after fix: package-update code suspends only an actually enabled Scheduled Task, resumes only if this update suspended it, and surfaces recovery failure if stop fails after suspension.

What was not tested: full seeded native Windows package update with a legacy PT3M repeated Scheduled Task remains unproven because Azure Crabbox native Windows capacity was unavailable, and the GitHub Windows runner only proves native Windows/WSL capability rather than running the seeded package-update smoke. WSL2 execution inside a real distro remains blocked because the available Blacksmith Windows runner cannot create the WSL2 VM/distro.

target.title: fix(doctor): auto-repair stale session snapshot paths on --fix
target.body:
## Summary

Add `shouldRepair` support to `noteSessionSnapshotHealth()` so that `openclaw doctor --fix` can rewrite stale session snapshot paths in `sessions.json` instead of only reporting them as warnings.

Fixes #85689

## Problem

After a pnpm update changes the runtime root (e.g. `openclaw@2026.5.20_@types+express@5.0.6` → `openclaw@2026.5.20`), sessions that cached `skillsSnapshot.prompt` paths referencing the old root become stale. `openclaw doctor` correctly detects this and reports findings with both `cachedPath` and `expectedPath`, but:

1. `noteSessionSnapshotHealth()` has no `shouldRepair` parameter — it's always report-only
2. The update flow runs `doctor --fix` post-update, but session snapshot paths are never repaired
3. Users must manually edit `sessions.json` or reset affected sessions (losing history)

## Fix

The repair is **layered on top of the existing scanner** — all current scanner logic is preserved:

1. `extractBundledSkillRelativeSegments` — identifies stale bundled runtime skill paths
2. `resolveExpectedBundledSkillPath` — computes correct expected path with `pathExists` validation
3. `loadSessionStoreForSnapshotScan` — hydrates `skillsSnapshot.promptRef` blobbed values before scanning
4. `collectResolvedSkillPaths` — scans both `filePath` and `baseDir` fields
5. `collectCachedSnapshotPaths` — covers `skillsSnapshot.prompt`, `resolvedSkills`, and `injectedWorkspaceFiles`

The only additions are:
- `replacePathsInSession()` — scoped replacement that modifies only snapshot metadata fields
- `shouldRepair?: boolean` parameter on `noteSessionSnapshotHealth()`
- Repair block that reuses scanner findings and applies replacements
- `.bak` backup + `writeTextAtomic` for safe writes

**Key design: scoped replacement** — the repair parses `sessions.json`, finds the specific session by key, and replaces paths only in the affected fields (`skillsSnapshot.prompt`, `resolvedSkills[].filePath/baseDir`, `injectedWorkspaceFiles[].path`). Unrelated content (transcripts, user messages, other fields) is never modified.

## Changes

- `src/commands/doctor-session-snapshots.ts`: +168 lines (replacePathsInSession + repair block)
- `src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts`: +1 line (pass shouldRepair)
- `repro-doctor-session-snapshot-repair.mjs`: standalone proof script (6 scenarios)

## Path Replacement Strategy

The repair handles three forms of stale paths in sessions.json:

1. **JSON-escaped** (Windows backslashes, unicode): `JSON.stringify().slice(1,-1)` generates the escaped form
2. **XML-escaped** (skillsSnapshot.prompt locations): `&` `<` `>` `"` `'` entity encoding
3. **Raw unescaped** (fallback for non-encoded storage)

**Scoped replacement:** Only modifies the specific session's snapshot metadata fields. Unrelated content (transcripts, user messages, other fields) is preserved unchanged.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** `openclaw doctor --fix` reports stale session snapshot paths but never repairs them. After a pnpm runtime root change (e.g. `openclaw@2026.5.20_@types+express@5.0.6` → `openclaw@2026.5.20`), sessions.json caches paths that no longer exist. The production `openclaw doctor --fix` is report-only — it detects stale paths but does not rewrite them. This PR adds `shouldRepair` support that rewrites stale paths in both inline prompts and promptRef blobs.

**Real environment tested:** Linux x64, Node v24.14.0. Live production instance with 20 sessions. Production `openclaw doctor` run with synthetic stale paths injected (simulating pnpm runtime root change). Standalone proof script mirrors the exact repair logic from the PR, operating on real sessions.json data.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
```bash
node proof-doctor-session-snapshot-repair.mjs
```

**Evidence after fix:**

**1. Production `openclaw doctor` — detects 20 stale sessions (BEFORE):**
```
Session snapshots
- Found 20 sessions with stale cached session metadata paths.
  Live bundled skills root is healthy:
  ~/.local/share/pnpm/global/5/.pnpm/openclaw@2026.5.20/...
  Cached session metadata still references an inactive runtime root;
  start a fresh session or reset the affected long-lived sessions
  after confirming history can be retired.
  Store: /tmp/doctor-proof-test/agents/main/sessions/sessions.json
  - agent:main:main skillsSnapshot.prompt: ...
```

**2. Production `openclaw doctor --fix` — still report-only (confirms current main does NOT repair):**
```
Session snapshots
- Found 20 sessions with stale cached session metadata paths.
  (same output — no repair performed)
```

**3. Standalone proof script — all 20 sessions repaired (AFTER):**
```
Real Behavior Proof: Doctor Session Snapshot Auto-Repair
Date: 2026-05-30T17:04:00.000Z
Node: v24.14.0 | Platform: linux x64

Real sessions.json: 20 sessions
Sessions with bundled runtime skill paths: 20
Injected stale paths into 20 sessions (simulating pnpm runtime root change)

Scenario 1: openclaw doctor (report only)
  Sessions with stale bundled paths: 20
  Repair performed: NO
  PASS: stale paths detected, no repair

Scenario 2: openclaw doctor --fix (shouldRepair=true)
  Sessions repaired: 20
  Stale paths remaining: 0
  JSON valid: true
  PASS: all stale paths replaced

Scenario 3: Re-scan after repair
  Leftover findings: 0
  PASS: no stale paths remain

Scenario 4: Scoped replacement
  Sample session: agent:main:main
  Prompt contains stale paths: false
  PASS: prompt repaired, unrelated fields preserved

SUMMARY
  Real sessions analyzed: 20
  Sessions with stale paths injected: 20
  Sessions repaired: 20
  Stale paths remaining: 0
  Leftover findings: 0
```

**Observed result after fix:** The production `openclaw doctor` correctly detects 20 sessions with stale bundled skill paths. The production `openclaw doctor --fix` remains report-only (confirms the issue). The proof script, which mirrors the exact repair logic from the PR, successfully repairs all 20 sessions: inline prompt paths, promptRef blob paths (with new blob hash), and resolvedSkills filePath/baseDir paths. After repair, re-scan finds 0 stale paths. Scoped replacement preserves unrelated session content.

**What was not tested:**
- Live `openclaw doctor --fix` with the patched binary (local build fails due to missing native bindings in container environment)
- Concurrent access to sessions.json during repair (single-threaded doctor execution)

**Proof coverage:**
The standalone script operates on the real 20-session sessions.json from the running instance:
- Same repair logic as `src/commands/doctor-session-snapshots.ts`: raw JSON targeted replacement
- PromptRef blobs: reads blob, replaces paths, writes new blob, updates hash/bytes
- Inline prompts: replaces directly in raw JSON
- Scoped: only snapshot metadata fields modified
- Idempotent: re-scan after repair finds 0 stale paths

commands.author: luoyanglang

target.title: fix(update): fail closed on managed plugin pin conflicts
target.body:
`openclaw update` could silently downgrade a user-pinned managed plugin direct dependency. After the maintainer policy note, this PR now implements the conflict/fail-closed behavior instead of preserving the newer pin automatically.

Fixes #85184.

## Affected surface

- `src/plugins/install.ts`
  - `installPluginFromManagedNpmRoot`
  - managed npm update-mode conflict handling
- `src/infra/npm-managed-root.ts`
  - `upsertManagedNpmRootDependency`
  - release-managed dependency spec writing
- Tests:
  - `src/plugins/install.npm-spec.test.ts`
  - `src/infra/npm-managed-root.test.ts`

## Scope

- Release-managed plugin versions remain authoritative for the managed npm root write path.
- In explicit update mode, if the managed npm project already has a newer direct dependency pin than the release-managed target, the update fails closed instead of downgrading or silently preserving it.
- The conflict is detected before `npm install`, including the case where the package payload is missing and `resolveEffectiveInstallMode()` would otherwise fall back from update to install.
- The error names the package, current package.json pin, release target, and the operator choices: use release-managed version, keep the user pin explicitly, or abort.
- Existing install mode, equal versions, older managed versions, peer repair, and unrelated dependency updates keep the existing behavior.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: An update-mode managed npm plugin install now refuses to automatically choose between a user-pinned newer direct dependency and the release-managed target.
- Real environment tested: macOS local development machine, current PR head `851c6d9f1388`, real OpenClaw source imported with `node --import tsx`, real npm metadata lookup for `@martian-engineering/lossless-claw@0.10.0`, temporary managed npm root outside the repository.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Create a temporary managed npm project with `package.json` pinning `@martian-engineering/lossless-claw` to `0.11.2`; run `installPluginFromNpmSpec(..., { spec: "@martian-engineering/lossless-claw@0.10.0", mode: "update" })`; verify the result fails closed, the package.json pin remains `0.11.2`, and no npm install artifacts are created.
- Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "proof": "OpenClaw PR #87304 managed plugin pin conflict proof",
```

target.title: fix(update): fail closed on managed plugin pin conflicts
target.body:
`openclaw update` could silently downgrade a user-pinned managed plugin direct dependency. After the maintainer policy note, this PR now implements the conflict/fail-closed behavior instead of preserving the newer pin automatically.

Fixes #85184.

## Affected surface

- `src/plugins/install.ts`
  - `installPluginFromManagedNpmRoot`
  - managed npm update-mode conflict handling
- `src/infra/npm-managed-root.ts`
  - `upsertManagedNpmRootDependency`
  - release-managed dependency spec writing
- Tests:
  - `src/plugins/install.npm-spec.test.ts`
  - `src/infra/npm-managed-root.test.ts`

## Scope

- Release-managed plugin versions remain authoritative for the managed npm root write path.
- In explicit update mode, if the managed npm project already has a newer direct dependency pin than the release-managed target, the update fails closed instead of downgrading or silently preserving it.
- The conflict is detected before `npm install`, including the case where the package payload is missing and `resolveEffectiveInstallMode()` would otherwise fall back from update to install.
- The error names the package, current package.json pin, release target, and the operator choices: use release-managed version, keep the user pin explicitly, or abort.
- Existing install mode, equal versions, older managed versions, peer repair, and unrelated dependency updates keep the existing behavior.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: An update-mode managed npm plugin install now refuses to automatically choose between a user-pinned newer direct dependency and the release-managed target.
- Real environment tested: macOS local development machine, current PR head `851c6d9f1388`, real OpenClaw source imported with `node --import tsx`, real npm metadata lookup for `@martian-engineering/lossless-claw@0.10.0`, temporary managed npm root outside the repository.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Create a temporary managed npm project with `package.json` pinning `@martian-engineering/lossless-claw` to `0.11.2`; run `installPluginFromNpmSpec(..., { spec: "@martian-engineering/lossless-claw@0.10.0", mode: "update" })`; verify the result fails closed, the package.json pin remains `0.11.2`, and no npm install artifacts are created.
- Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "proof": "OpenClaw PR #87304 managed plugin pin conflict proof",
  "head": "851c6d9f1388",
  "packageName": "@martian-engineering/lossless-claw",
  "requestedUpdateSpec": "@martian-engineering/lossless-claw@0.10.0",
  "existingPackageJsonPin": "0.11.2",
  "result": {
    "ok": false,
    "error": "Managed plugin dependency conflict for @martian-engineering/lossless-claw: current package.json pin is 0.11.2 but OpenClaw release target is 0.10.0. Non-interactive update is refusing to choose automatically. Use the release-managed version, keep the user pin explicitly, or abort."
  },
  "finalPackageJsonPin": "0.11.2",
  "npmInstallRan": false,
  "tempRoot": "<tmp>/openclaw-pr87304-policy-proof-hlkUGi"
}
```

Local validation after the rewrite:

```text
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.plugins.config.ts src/plugins/install.npm-spec.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  45 passed (45)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.infra.config.ts src/infra/npm-managed-root.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  19 passed (19)

$ ./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check src/plugins/install.ts src/plugins/install.npm-spec.test.ts src/infra/npm-managed-root.ts src/infra/npm-managed-root.test.ts
All matched files use the correct format.

$ git diff --check && git diff --check origin/main..HEAD && node scripts/check-no-conflict-markers.mjs
(exit 0)

$ gitleaks protect --staged --redact
no leaks found
```

`pnpm check:changed --base origin/main` currently reaches the repo guards and then fails in core typecheck on `src/gateway/server-session-key.ts(55,5)`. This file is unchanged by this PR and matches current `origin/main`; I left that baseline issue out of this policy PR.

- Observed result after fix: The update returns an actionable conflict error, leaves the user pin unchanged, and does not run npm install.
- What was not tested: I did not implement an interactive chooser in this patch. The current install boundary is non-interactive, so this PR establishes the fail-closed behavior and error shape that an interactive update flow can later use.

## Coverage note

This remains the open PR for #85184. Codegraph-reported overlap with #86122, #87477, and #87573 is in the shared plugin install/update functions; the policy behavior above should remain the compatibility point for those edits.

commands.author: Jerry-Xin

target.title: fix(tts): defer text settlement for final-mode TTS to eliminate churn (#83511)
target.body:
## Summary

When `messages.tts.mode = "final"`, the Telegram bot exhibited text "churn" — users would briefly see a text message appear, then it would be deleted and replaced by a voice note with the same text as caption. This happened because two independent delivery paths were active simultaneously:

1. **Draft stream path**: `onPartialReply` → `bot.api.sendMessage()` sent preview text immediately
2. **ACP projector path**: `suppressBlockUserDelivery` blocked ACP block delivery but couldn't reach the upstream draft stream
3. **TTS finalization**: voice note with caption sent after synthesis, then draft text deleted

This PR eliminates the churn by suppressing draft stream creation at source when captioned final TTS will be used.

## Root cause

```
Before (main):
  1. onPartialReply fires → draft stream creates sendMessage preview → user sees text
  2. TTS synthesis runs (2-5 seconds)
  3. Voice note + caption delivered → draft text deleted
  Result: user sees text flash then disappear = churn (#83511)

After (this PR):
  1. resolveCaptionedFinalTtsPlan() → willUseCaptionedFinalTts = true
  2. canStreamAnswerDraft = false → NO draft stream created
  3. TTS synthesis runs silently
  4. Voice note + caption delivered as single message
  Result: user sees only the voice note with caption = no churn
```

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/tts/captioned-final-tts.ts` | **New** shared helper `resolveCaptionedFinalTtsPlan()` combining channel capability, TTS mode, and TTS status checks |
| `src/tts/captioned-final-tts.test.ts` | **New** 4 unit tests for the shared helper |
| `src/plugin-sdk/tts-runtime.ts` | Re-export `resolveCaptionedFinalTtsPlan` for extension access |
| `docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256` | Updated SDK API baseline hash |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts` | Refactored to use shared helper instead of inline TTS logic; removed `channelTtsCapabilitiesRuntimeLoader` |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts` | 4 regression tests for deferred settlement, block suppression, abort fallback |
| `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.ts` | Added `isTelegramInboundAudioContext()`, `resolveTelegramSessionTtsAuto()`, and `!willUseCaptionedFinalTts` guard on `canStreamAnswerDraft` |
| `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts` | 2 new tests: draft suppression when captioned TTS active, normal streaming when not |
| `extensions/telegram/src/shared.ts` | `captionedFinalText` capability declaration |
| `extensions/telegram/src/shared.test.ts` | Test for capability declaration |
| `src/channels/plugins/types.core.ts` | JSDoc for `captionedFinalText` capability contract |
| `src/channels/plugins/tts-capabilities.test.ts` | Capability seam test |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/acp-projector.ts` | Block delivery suppression support |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.ts` | Delivery coordinator for suppressed text fallback |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts
 ✓ 58 passed (58)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/tts/captioned-final-tts.test.ts
 ✓ 4 passed (4)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts
 ✓ 96 passed (96)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/shared.test.ts
 ✓ 11 passed (11)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/channels/plugins/tts-capabilities.test.ts
 ✓ 1 passed (1)

$ pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check
 OK docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256

Total: 170 tests passed across 5 test suites
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Telegram TTS final-mode "delete-and-resend churn" — when TTS mode is `final`, users saw a text message flash briefly (2-5 seconds), then it disappeared and a voice note with caption appeared. Root cause: two independent delivery paths (draft stream via `onPartialReply` and ACP block suppression via `suppressBlockUserDelivery`) operated at different layers, so the draft stream was never suppressed.

- **Real environment tested**: Local patched OpenClaw checkout at commit `d670d829`, macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0. Live Telegram bot (`@thomas_fu_bot`) with Microsoft TTS (`zh-CN-XiaoxiaoNeural`), `messages.tts.mode: "final"`, `messages.tts.auto: "always"`, `channels.telegram.streaming.mode: "block"`.

- **Failing proof before fix (live Telegram)**:

  ![Before fix — separate text message + voice message (churn)](https://cdn.deepminer.com.cn/im-test-xming/chat/5/f6138257d34c4372bf6b6143e44cb3f5____2060648631760850944/48AAB56AD0D1DC685CB3918F75AEB402)

  User sent "hello" to the Telegram bot. The bot responded with **two separate messages**: a text message ("老板好，好久不见！有什么需要效劳的？") AND a voice note — instead of a single voice note with caption. This is the churn behavior described in #83511: the draft stream sends text via `sendMessage` immediately, then TTS synthesis completes and sends a separate voice note.

- **Exact steps after this patch**:
  1. Applied patch (commit `d670d829`) via `npm link`
  2. Restarted Gateway: `openclaw gateway restart`
  3. Sent messages from Telegram to the bot
  4. Observed: voice note with caption appeared directly — no text flash

- **Evidence after fix (live Telegram screenshot)**:

  ![After fix — voice messages with caption, no text churn](https://cdn.deepminer.com.cn/im-test-xming/chat/5/f6138257d34c4372bf6b6143e44cb3f5____2060648631760850944/6937B66CC8A27C33B76FBD54155B542C)

  The screenshot shows two voice messages from the bot, each with text caption in the same bubble. No separate text messages were sent — the text is delivered as caption on the voice note, exactly as intended.

- **Observed result after fix**: Voice notes are delivered as a single message with text caption. No draft text message is created or deleted. The `canStreamAnswerDraft` flag is correctly set to `false` when captioned final TTS is planned, preventing draft stream creation at source. Non-TTS conversations continue to use normal streaming. Other channels (Discord, Slack, etc.) are unaffected.

- **What was not tested**: Other TTS providers (only Microsoft TTS tested live). Group chat TTS behavior (tested in DM only). The `auto=inbound` edge case was verified by unit tests but not live-tested.

Closes #83511

commands.author: JOY

target.title: feat(zalouser): surface inbound photo attachments via media array
target.body:
## Summary

Surfaces inbound photo attachments on the Zalo personal-account channel as native vision content blocks. Previously the `zalouser` monitor handler dropped Zalo photo metadata, so agents only saw the user's text caption and could not respond to image content. After this PR an inbound Zalo photo lands on the agent's turn as a kernel vision block, and the model can reason about the image directly.

## What changes

- `extensions/zalouser/src/zalo-js.ts`: new `extractInboundMedia(content)` helper that recognises photo content payloads from `zca-js` events. Detects via `type === 'photo'`, image-extension URL pattern, or non-empty `thumb`. Returns `{ kind: 'image', url, thumbUrl? }` for downstream pickup.
- `extensions/zalouser/src/zalo-js.ts`: `toInboundMessage` returns a new `media` field populated from `extractInboundMedia`.
- `extensions/zalouser/src/monitor.ts`: new `resolveInboundImageContentType(detected, url)` helper that trusts a real `image/*` MIME when the CDN returns one, otherwise maps the URL extension (`.jpg|.png|.gif|.webp|.bmp`) to the correct image MIME. Falls back to `image/jpeg` when both signals are absent. Closes the Zalo-CDN-returns-`application/octet-stream` bug where the kernel rejected the photo because `MediaType` did not start with `image/`.
- `extensions/zalouser/src/monitor.ts`: when an inbound message carries a `media` payload, fetch the photo (via existing `saveRemoteMedia` plumbing), persist to the agent volume, and wire `{ path, url, contentType }` into the `core.channel.turn.buildContext({ media: [...] })` call. The kernel auto-derives `MediaPath/MediaUrl/MediaType` so the photo reaches the model as a native vision block.
- `extensions/zalouser/src/monitor.ts`: relax the empty-body guard so a photo-only message (zero text content) is not dropped.

## Tests

- `extensions/zalouser/src/monitor.media.test.ts` - new test file for `resolveInboundImageContentType` with 7 cases covering happy-path image/* trust, Zalo CDN `application/octet-stream` override, URL extension fallback, case-insensitivity, query-string stripping, unknown extensions, and the missing-both fallback to `image/jpeg`.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: Inbound Zalo photo attachments arriving via the `@openclaw/zalouser` channel were silently dropped by `monitor.ts` - the model saw only the user's text caption, never the image. Two compounding bugs: (1) `toInboundMessage` did not surface the photo content at all, (2) the Zalo CDN returns `Content-Type: application/octet-stream` for photos, so even when fetched the kernel's `resolveCurrentTurnImages` rejected the file because `MediaType` did not start with `image/`.

**Real environment tested**: Em Huyen agent (Tingee customer-support bot, Vietnamese) running on the openclaw runtime with `@openclaw/zalouser` paired to a real Zalo personal account, talking to a real Zalo group room (Capypara.AI). Channel `zalouser`, model `dos-ai/dos-ai` (Qwen3.6-35B-a3b). Tested on both Zalo Mobile (Android) and Zalo Desktop on the same conversation.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
1. Apply this PR's `zalo-js.ts` + `monitor.ts` changes to the running zalouser plugin.
2. Restart the agent container so the plugin reloads.
3. From a separate Zalo account, send a photo of a Tingee soundbox bottom plate to the Capypara.AI group with the text `@Em Huyền reset loa cho anh`. The plate has a printed sticker showing `Model: Tingee 3PRO`, `SN: 3PRO175459`.
4. Wait for Em Huyen to reply.
5. Open the same conversation on Zalo Android and on Zalo Desktop; screenshot the inbound photo + bot reply.

**Evidence after fix**:

Zalo Android (Capypara.AI group, dark theme):

![Em Huyen extracts SN from inbound photo on Zalo Android](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/JOY/openclaw/proof/zalouser-em-huyen-screenshots/docs/proof/zalouser-em-huyen/zalo-mobile.jpg)

Zalo Desktop (same conversation):

![Em Huyen extracts SN from inbound photo on Zalo Desktop](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/JOY/openclaw/proof/zalouser-em-huyen-screenshots/docs/proof/zalouser-em-huyen/zalo-desktop.jpg)

**Observed result after fix**: Em Huyen's reply opens with `Dạ anh Le, em đã nhìn rõ ảnh rồi. Mã thiết bị của anh là: SN: 3PRO175459 (Model: Tingee 3PRO).` That SN string is printed on the physical label in the photo - the agent extracted it directly from the inbound vision block, not from prior context. Confirms end-to-end that (a) `extractInboundMedia` recognised the Zalo photo content payload, (b) `resolveInboundImageContentType` overrode the Zalo CDN's `application/octet-stream` to `image/jpeg`, (c) the kernel vision pipeline accepted the photo, (d) the agent received the image as a native vision block and read the SN off the label.

**What was not tested**: I have not run a head-to-head BEFORE / AFTER screenshot pair in the same Zalo conversation - the pre-fix behavior was the live deployment state captured separately in the downstream vendor patch's tracking ticket, which is what motivated this PR. The AFTER screenshots above are from a real deployment with the fix in place. Maintainers can reproduce the BEFORE state by reverting `extensions/zalouser/src/monitor.ts` to the pre-PR version and re-sending the same photo - the bot will reply without referencing the SN because it never received the vision block.

Screenshots live on a separate `proof/zalouser-em-huyen-screenshots` branch on the fork so the upstream merge does not import binaries into openclaw history.

## Refs

Companion outbound PR: openclaw/openclaw#85039 (Zalo client markdown normalizer).

target.title: fix(ui): add agent selector to dreaming tab
target.body:
## Summary

- Closes #63558.
- The Control UI Dreaming tab needs to work across agent contexts. Without a selector and scoped reloads, users could switch visible context but still see the default agent's dreaming status, diary, or related memory state.
- This PR adds Dreaming agent selection, scopes Dreaming status/diary reloads to the selected agent, and keeps backward-compatible default-agent fallback when no agent id is supplied.
- It also opens report palace cards as wiki previews from Dreaming so report links stay in the Control UI review flow.
- Out of scope: changing Dreaming storage semantics, changing auth/network/tool execution behavior, or broad Control UI routing refactors.
- Review focus: selected-agent propagation through the Dreaming UI/controller/gateway path, report-card preview routing, and the generated i18n updates once the separate merge-conflict item is resolved.

## Linked context

Closes #63558.

Related context:
- This PR is a targeted Control UI/Dreaming fix for agent-specific Dreaming context.
- The current branch also has a separate tracked upstream merge conflict in generated Control UI i18n metadata/translation files; that conflict is not part of this body-only update.

Maintainer/owner request:
- No separate maintainer request beyond the linked issue and PR review automation.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Dreaming lacked reliable selected-agent behavior. Agent selection in the Dreaming tab needed to scope `dreamingStatus` and `dreamDiary` loads to the selected agent instead of falling back to the default context.
- Real environment tested: local `openclaw/openclaw` checkout on Linux, Node.js v24.14.0, vitest v4.1.5.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-methods.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/doctor.test.ts`
  - Earlier UI proof on this PR: `cd ui && pnpm vitest run src/ui/views/dreaming.test.ts --reporter=verbose`
  - Earlier support checks on this PR: `pnpm tsgo:core` and `pnpm lint:core`
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

~~~text
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-methods.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/doctor.test.ts

 RUN  v4.1.5 /tmp/pincer-openclaw

 ✓  gateway-methods  src/gateway/server-methods/doctor.test.ts (27 tests) 89ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  27 passed (27)
    Start at  00:25:12
    Duration  470ms
~~~

~~~text
✓ src/ui/views/dreaming.test.ts > dreaming view > renders an agent selector and switches agent contexts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       16 passed (16)
~~~

- Observed result after fix: `onSelectAgent` records the selected agent id, Dreaming reloads status and diary data scoped to that agent, gateway handlers accept optional `agentId`, and omitted `agentId` continues to resolve through the default-agent fallback.
- What was not tested: live browser interaction against a running multi-agent gateway.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: this update only repairs the PR body template. The branch still has a separate generated i18n merge-tree conflict against current `upstream/main`, so this run did not push code or rerun the full branch validation suite.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): before the fix, Dreaming selection could call the session switch path while the subsequent `doctor.memory.*` loads still used `{}`, leaving the tab on default-agent Dreaming data.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

For this body repair:
- Fetched the current upstream `.github/pull_request_template.md` for the PR base branch through the authenticated GitHub API.
- Updated the PR body through the authenticated GitHub API.
- Re-read the live PR body through the authenticated GitHub API and verified every current top-level template heading is present.

Existing code validation recorded for this PR:
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-methods.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/doctor.test.ts`
- `cd ui && pnpm vitest run src/ui/views/dreaming.test.ts --reporter=verbose`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`
- `pnpm lint:core`

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- `ui/src/ui/views/dreaming.test.ts` covers rendering the agent selector and changing agent context.
- `ui/src/ui/controllers/dreaming.test.ts` covers Dreaming controller selected-agent behavior.
- `src/gateway/server-methods/doctor.test.ts` covers optional agent-id handling and default fallback for the gateway methods.
- `ui/src/ui/app-settings.refresh-active-tab.node.test.ts` covers active-tab refresh state touched by the Dreaming flow.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- Dreaming did not expose the same agent-selection control expected in multi-agent Control UI contexts.
- Selected-agent changes could leave Dreaming status/diary loads scoped to the default agent rather than the chosen agent.

If no test was added, why not?

- N/A. The branch includes focused UI/controller/gateway regression coverage. This body-only worker item did not add code.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

What is the highest-risk area?

- Showing Dreaming data for the wrong agent, or routing report/wiki preview interactions through the wrong Control UI context.

How is that risk mitigated?

- The selector and scoped reload paths are covered by focused Dreaming UI/controller tests and gateway method tests.
- Omitted `agentId` remains backward compatible through the existing default-agent fallback.

## Current review state

- PR body template compliance is addressed by this update.
- The remaining branch action is the already-tracked generated Control UI i18n conflict/current-head check failure, not this body-only item.

commands.author: Jerry-Xin

target.title: fix(gateway): strip spurious tool call blocks when provider signals stop
target.body:
## Summary

Strip spurious tool call content blocks from OpenAI-compatible completions streaming responses when the provider signals `finish_reason: "stop"` (not `"tool_calls"`).

## Problem

When an OpenAI-compatible provider (e.g., llama.cpp serving Qwen3.6 with thinking enabled) emits structured `delta.tool_calls` parsed from the model's reasoning/thinking output but correctly reports `finish_reason: "stop"`, the gateway unconditionally builds `toolCall` content blocks and the agent loop executes them.

This is caused by an asymmetric stop-reason guard in `processOpenAICompletionsStream`: the existing guard downgrades `stopReason` from `"toolUse"` to `"stop"` when no tool call blocks are present (line 2679-2681), but the inverse guard — stripping tool call blocks when `stopReason` is not `"toolUse"` — was missing.

## Fix

Add the missing inverse guard after the existing one:

```typescript
if (hasToolCalls && output.stopReason !== "toolUse") {
  output.content = output.content.filter((block) => block.type !== "toolCall");
}
```

This ensures that when a provider does not signal tool-use intent via `finish_reason`, any tool call blocks in the response are treated as spurious and discarded.

## Tests

Added three test cases to `openai-transport-stream.test.ts`:
1. `delta.tool_calls` + `finish_reason: "stop"` → tool call blocks stripped ✅
2. `delta.tool_calls` + `finish_reason: "tool_calls"` → tool calls preserved (regression) ✅
3. No tool calls + `finish_reason: "stop"` → no change (regression) ✅

All 354 tests pass.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: OpenAI-compatible completions streaming responses containing `delta.tool_calls` with `finish_reason: "stop"` (instead of `"tool_calls"`) cause the gateway to execute spurious tool calls. This occurs when providers (e.g., older llama.cpp, vLLM, or other OpenAI-compatible backends) do not properly separate reasoning/thinking content from tool call output. Both the gateway transport parser (`openai-transport-stream.ts`) and the registered provider parser (`openai-completions.ts`) were vulnerable.

- **Real environment tested**:
  - **Before-fix SSE capture**: Native `llama-server` compiled from ggml-org/llama.cpp master (commit `0f3cb3f`), `cmake -DGGML_CUDA=ON -DCMAKE_CUDA_ARCHITECTURES=80`, on NVIDIA A100-PCIE-40GB. Two models: Qwen3-0.6B-Q8_0.gguf (single A100, GPU 5) and Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-Q4_K_M.gguf (21GB, 3× A100 GPU 5/6/7). Thinking mode enabled.
  - **After-fix Docker gateway**: Docker image `openclaw-pr85248:test` built from PR HEAD (commit `d02abc31`), Linux arm64, Node.js v24.14.0. Mock SSE replay server feeding real llama-server SSE data (96 chunks, Qwen3-0.6B, A100) with `finish_reason` set to `"stop"` to reproduce the exact bug scenario.
  - **Unit tests**: `npx vitest run` in PR checkout, 421 tests across both parsers.

- **Failing proof before fix**: On upstream/main, `openai-transport-stream.ts:2678-2681` only has a one-way guard (`toolUse` → `stop` when no tool-call blocks exist). The inverse guard (strip tool-call blocks when `stopReason !== "toolUse"`) was missing. The registered provider parser `openai-completions.ts` had no guard at all. Source-verified at both locations on current main.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. Compiled llama-server from ggml-org/llama.cpp (commit `0f3cb3f`) with CUDA on A100
  2. Captured 96 real SSE chunks from Qwen3-0.6B with tool definitions + thinking mode
  3. Modified `finish_reason` from `"tool_calls"` to `"stop"` to reproduce the exact mismatch scenario
  4. Built Docker image from PR branch: `docker build -t openclaw-pr85248:test .`
  5. Started mock SSE replay server on host (Node.js HTTP, port 9998)
  6. Ran Docker gateway with `--allow-unconfigured`, provider pointed at mock server via `host.docker.internal:9998`
  7. Triggered isolated cron job: `{"kind": "agentTurn", "message": "Use the exec tool to run: echo hello"}`
  8. Captured gateway logs: `docker logs proof-85248 2>&1 | grep -i -E "tool|strip|stop|finish|reasoning"`
  9. Ran unit tests: `npx vitest run src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts src/llm/providers/openai-completions.test.ts`

- **Evidence after fix**:
  ```
  [agent/embedded] reasoning-only assistant turn detected: provider=llama-mock/Qwen3-0.6B-Q8_0.gguf — retrying 1/2 with visible-answer continuation
  [agent/embedded] reasoning-only assistant turn detected: provider=llama-mock/Qwen3-0.6B-Q8_0.gguf — retrying 2/2 with visible-answer continuation
  [agent/embedded] reasoning-only retries exhausted: attempts=2/2 — surfacing incomplete-turn error
  ```
  - Zero `tool start` / `tool end` log entries — `exec({"command": "echo hello"})` was stripped
  - Gateway classified response as "reasoning-only" (only reasoning_content remained after stripping)
  - No tool was executed; graceful degradation to incomplete-turn error
  - Unit tests: 421 passed (3 new openai-completions + 209×2 openai-transport-stream)
  ```
  ✓ unit openai-completions.test.ts (3 tests) 67ms
  ✓ agents-core openai-transport-stream.test.ts (209 tests) 183ms
  ✓ agents-support openai-transport-stream.test.ts (209 tests) 166ms
  Test Files  3 passed (3)
       Tests  421 passed (421)
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: The inverse guard `hasToolCalls && stopReason !== "toolUse"` correctly strips spurious tool call blocks in both parsers when the provider does not signal tool-use intent. The Docker gateway log confirms no tool execution occurred. Additionally, Qwen3.6-35B-A3B testing on the same llama-server confirmed that current llama.cpp (commit `0f3cb3f`) correctly separates `reasoning_content` from `tool_calls` and does not naturally trigger the bug — the guard is a defensive fix protecting against other OpenAI-compatible backends that do not properly separate reasoning output.

- **What was not tested**: Live end-to-end integration with a provider that naturally produces the exact mismatch (current llama.cpp master correctly separates reasoning, so the bug was reproduced via SSE replay with modified `finish_reason`). The `finish_reason: "length"` + accumulated tool calls edge case is covered by unit tests but not by Docker runtime proof.

target.title: fix(agents): recover DeepSeek DSML tool calls
target.body:
Fixes #85918

## Summary

- Recover complete DeepSeek DSML invoke blocks on the OpenAI-compatible completions stream when tools were actually exposed and the invoke name matches a declared tool.
- Preserve existing DSML visible-text stripping behavior, keep native `tool_calls` authoritative, and cap retained DSML bodies.
- Add regression coverage for parameter semantics, split chunks, native-shadow de-dupe/replacement, same-chunk native replacement ordering, trailing text ordering, disabled/unknown tools, malformed DSML, tool-choice narrowing, structured thinking, and oversized DSML bodies.

## Canonicalization note

This PR intentionally supersedes the overlapping DSML recovery shape in #86637:

- #88320 is rebased on current `upstream/main` and includes a packaged/local OpenClaw CLI proof rather than only unit-level proof.
- It keeps native provider `tool_calls` authoritative and covers native-shadow de-duplication/replacement, including delayed native chunks and same-chunk visible-content ordering.
- It includes broader parser/transport coverage for malformed, incomplete, unknown, disabled, split, typed-parameter, zero-argument, and oversized DSML cases.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: DeepSeek/Foundry completions streams that emit complete DSML tool-call markup as assistant text now become executable OpenClaw tool calls instead of visible/filtered text, while native `tool_calls` remain authoritative.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on this PR branch, rebased onto current `upstream/main`, executed through the real OpenClaw CLI via `node scripts/run-node.mjs --profile pr88320proof agent --local ... --json`. The model provider was an isolated OpenAI-compatible loopback SSE server configured as `dsml-loopback/deepseek-v4-pro` with DeepSeek compatibility enabled. No live provider credentials were used.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Built/reran the local OpenClaw CLI from this branch, configured an isolated `pr88320proof` profile to point at `http://127.0.0.1:19191/v1`, started a local OpenAI-compatible SSE server that first returned the reported DSML `session_status` payload as assistant text, then ran:
`node scripts/run-node.mjs --profile pr88320proof agent --local --agent main --session-key agent:main:dsml-proof-<fresh> --model dsml-loopback/deepseek-v4-pro --message 'Call the session_status tool exactly once with sessionKey current and then stop.' --json --timeout 90`

Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "exitCode": 0,
  "head": "a97c6fb",
  "firstProviderRequest": {
    "url": "/v1/chat/completions",
    "model": "deepseek-v4-pro",
    "stream": true,
    "toolChoice": "auto",
    "toolNamesIncludesSessionStatus": true,
    "messages": ["system", "user"]
  },
  "loopbackFirstResponse": "<｜DSML｜tool_calls><｜DSML｜invoke name=\"session_status\"><｜DSML｜parameter name=\"sessionKey\" string=\"true\">current</｜DSML｜parameter></｜DSML｜invoke></｜DSML｜tool_calls>",
  "secondProviderRequest": {
    "url": "/v1/chat/completions",
    "model": "deepseek-v4-pro",
    "stream": true,
    "containsToolRoleMessage": true
  },
  "sawToolResultRequest": true,
  "secondRequestHasToolRole": true,
  "dsmlLeakedToFinalStdout": false,
  "finalContainsProofDone": true
}
```

Observed result after fix: The first provider response returned DSML as assistant text, OpenClaw recovered it into an executable `session_status` tool call, the second provider request contained a `role: tool` message, the final CLI JSON completed successfully, and the DSML wrapper did not leak into final stdout.

What was not tested: A live Microsoft Foundry/DeepSeek provider call with real credentials. The proof uses the packaged/local OpenClaw CLI path from this branch plus a loopback OpenAI-compatible SSE provider that emits the exact reported DSML shape.

## Verification

target.title: fix(agents): skip below-target CLI compaction failures
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

- Prevents retried CLI/Codex agent turns from failing when compaction reports the transcript is already below target.
- Treats `already under target` / below-threshold compaction as a no-op instead of a fatal post-turn compaction error.

Why does this matter now?

- Telegram and other webhook retries can re-enter the same agent path after a transient turn failure. A harmless compaction no-op should not become a visible repeated error.

What is the intended outcome?

- CLI-backed Codex turns continue when compaction says there is nothing useful to reduce.
- Real compaction failures, such as timeouts, still fail loudly.

What is intentionally out of scope?

- Durable Telegram webhook dedupe behavior.
- Notification dispatch policy changes.
- Codex app-server compaction algorithm changes.

What does success look like?

- `already under target` compaction results resolve as no-ops.
- Existing timeout/native harness failure coverage still proves non-benign failures throw.

What should reviewers focus on?

- Whether `below_threshold` is the right existing classifier to reuse for CLI no-op behavior.
- Whether the no-op branch avoids clearing CLI resume state or recording a compaction count.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

- Requested during investigation of repeated Telegram-visible progress errors caused by retried CLI/Codex turns hitting `already under target` compaction.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: CLI/Codex compaction returning `compacted:false` with `reason:"already under target"` was treated as a fatal post-turn compaction failure.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS, Node 24.14.0, Codex CLI 0.135.0, Codex app-server 0.134.0.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a source-checkout Node/tsx smoke that creates an isolated production Codex app-server client, starts a real Codex thread, and calls `thread/compact/start` on that below-target thread.
- Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "proof": "PR88319 Codex app-server below-target compact smoke",
  "source": "local OpenClaw source checkout",
  "codexCli": "0.135.0",
  "serverVersion": "0.134.0",
  "threadStarted": true,
  "compactReturned": true,
  "visibleTurnFailure": false,
  "redactions": [
    "thread id",
    "temp path"
  ]
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The real Codex app-server compaction request returned successfully for the tiny below-target thread, and the OpenClaw regression tests prove both CLI transcript and Codex native `already under target` results now return as no-ops without recording a compaction or throwing.
- What was not tested: Live Telegram webhook retry against production bot credentials.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: The local Codex/OpenAI auth path hit a Cloudflare 403 when trying to run a full OpenClaw agent turn with context-engine fallback, so the real runtime proof is scoped to the production Codex app-server compaction request plus focused OpenClaw regression tests for the exact `already under target` reason.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): The pre-fix control flow threw for non-fallback `compacted:false` results; the old tests had no below-target coverage.


## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `PNPM_CONFIG_FETCH_RETRIES=5 PNPM_CONFIG_FETCH_RETRY_MINTIMEOUT=60000 PNPM_CONFIG_FETCH_RETRY_MAXTIMEOUT=180000 pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`
- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/command/cli-compaction.test.ts`
- `node_modules/.bin/tsgo -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.tsbuildinfo --declaration false --pretty false`
- `node_modules/.bin/tsgo -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo --declaration false --pretty false`
- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm check:changed` (blocked at unrelated lint tooling issue after core typechecks passed)

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Added context-engine CLI compaction coverage for `{ ok: true, compacted: false, reason: "already under target" }`.
- Added Codex native CLI compaction coverage for `{ ok: true, compacted: false, reason: "already under target" }`.
- Existing nonrecoverable native timeout failure coverage remains unchanged.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- The CLI compaction lifecycle treated non-fallback `compacted:false` as fatal, even when the reason was below-target/no-op.

If no test was added, why not?

- Tests were added.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. Harmless below-target compaction no-ops no longer surface as failed CLI/Codex turns.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

What is the highest-risk area?

- Accidentally swallowing real compaction failures.

How is that risk mitigated?

- The no-op handling is restricted to the existing `below_threshold` classifier.
- Timeout and unsupported/stale-binding failure paths remain covered separately.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

- Maintainer review and CI.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

- CI should confirm full repo gates in a clean environment.
- Optional live webhook verification can be done by a maintainer with Telegram credentials.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- None yet.
